content:
# Leviticus Chapter 13 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 65r–65v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_013_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_013_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 97–98 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_013_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_013_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 13 is the longest chapter in Leviticus at 59 verses, constituting the first half of the two-chapter tzara'at legislation (chs.13–14). The word tzara'at [צָרַעַת] is traditionally rendered "leprosy" in the KJV and most older translations, but this is almost certainly wrong in the modern medical sense: Hansen's disease (Mycobacterium leprae) was likely unknown in the ancient Levant in the Mosaic period, and the descriptions in ch.13 do not match its clinical presentation. The chapter covers a range of skin conditions — the criteria (changed color, spreading, depth of lesion, presence of raw flesh, etc.) suggest conditions such as vitiligo, psoriasis, fungal infections, ringworm, and various inflammatory dermatoses. Critically, the diagnosis is not medical but ritual: the priest (kohen) functions as examiner-adjudicator, not physician. His role is to determine ritual status (tameh/tahor), not to prescribe treatment. The chapter divides into seven diagnostic units: (1) vv.1–8: initial skin lesion examination; (2) vv.9–17: chronic/advanced tzara'at; (3) vv.18–23: boil (shechim) turning into tzara'at; (4) vv.24–28: burn (mikve'at-esh) turning into tzara'at; (5) vv.29–37: tzara'at of the head or beard (neteq); (6) vv.38–39: bright spots (baheret) — benign case; (7) vv.40–44: baldness (qarachah/gibbach) tzara'at. These seven units are followed by two appendices: vv.45–46 (the social behavior of the person declared tameh) and vv.47–59 (tzara'at in garments — extending the category beyond skin to textiles). The address is to both Moshe and Aharon (v.1), unlike ch.12 which was addressed to Moshe alone — the priestly diagnostic function makes Aharon's inclusion here essential. MT and English versification are identical throughout.]*

---

**1** And the LORD [YHWH] spoke [vayedabber] to Moshe [el-Moshe] and to Aharon [ve-el-Aharon] saying [lemor] [vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe ve-el-Aharon lemor — "And the LORD spoke to Moshe and to Aharon, saying"; this is the dual-address formula, reserved in Leviticus for legislation requiring active priestly involvement; ch.11 (food laws) also uses dual address because it requires the kohanim to teach and enforce the distinctions; ch.13 requires dual address because the kohen is himself the diagnostic agent — the examination and pronouncement are priestly acts, not merely things said to the people; the LXX renders this identically: Καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων λέγων (Kai elalēsen kyrios pros Mōysēn kai Aarōn legōn), "And the Lord spoke to Moses and Aaron, saying"]:

**2** "A person [adam] [adam — "a human being / a man"; adam is the most generic term for a human being in Hebrew, encompassing both sexes; it is used here rather than ish ("man") or ishah ("woman") because the laws of this chapter apply to all persons regardless of sex; LXX: ἄνθρωπος (anthrōpos, "a human being") — precise equivalent] when [ki] on the skin of his flesh [bi-vor basar] [bi-vor basar — "in the skin of his flesh / on/in the skin of his body"; bor is an alternative form of 'or ("skin") used in Leviticus specifically in the context of skin-disease diagnostics; it consistently appears in the idiom 'or basar ("skin of flesh") meaning the outer skin of the human body; the LXX renders bi-vor basar as ἐν τῷ δέρματι χρωτός αὐτοῦ (en tō dermati chrōtos autou, "in the skin of his body"), using δέρμα (derma, "skin") and χρώς (chrōs, "skin, body surface, complexion")] there is a swelling [se'et] [se'et — [swelling]; se'et is a technical term used only in the tzara'at diagnostic legislation of chs.13–14; from the root nasa' ("to lift, to raise"), it literally means "a raising / an elevation" — a skin lesion that rises above the surface; the LXX renders se'et as οὐλή (oulē, "scar, swelling, raised spot") in most occurrences, or sometimes as σημεῖον (sēmeion, "sign / mark")] or a rash [sapachat] [sapachat — [rash/scab]; sapachat is a skin lesion that appears to derive from sapach ("to attach, to adhere, to add on to"), suggesting an accretion or growth attached to the skin — a scab, rash, or crusty lesion; it may refer specifically to a flattened, adherent lesion as contrasted with the elevated se'et; LXX: θέρμα (therma, "swelling / heat-rash"), though some manuscripts have φλόγωσις (phlogōsis, "inflammation, flaming")] or a bright-spot [baheret] [baheret — [bright spot]; baheret is from bahar ("to be bright / to shine"), denoting a whitish, shining lesion — a patch of skin that has turned bright or pale relative to normal skin color; the three terms se'et, sapachat, and baheret appear together in v.2 as the three triggering presentations that require priestly examination; they likely represent a spectrum of lesion types: raised lesion, flat/adherent lesion, and shiny/pale lesion; LXX: τηλαύγημα (tēlaugēma, "something visible from afar / a bright shining mark") or λαμπρόν (lampron, "bright, shining") — consistent with the root sense of baheret as a shining mark], and it becomes on the skin of his flesh [ve-hayah be-'or-basar] a mark of tzara'at [le-nega tzara'at] [le-nega tzara'at — "for/as a mark/affliction of tzara'at"; nega is from naga' ("to strike, to touch"), meaning "a stroke, a blow, an affliction, a mark" — something that has struck or touched the skin; nega tzara'at is the technical phrase "mark/affliction of tzara'at," the formal designation of the suspected condition before the priest's verdict; LXX: ἁφὴ λέπρας (haphē lepras, "a touch/mark of leprosy") — using ἁφή (haphē, "touch, contact, a touching") for nega, and λέπρα (lepra, "leprosy") for tzara'at; the LXX's lepra gave the traditional rendering "leprosy" that has dominated Bible translations for two millennia; in classical Greek lepra referred to a scaly skin condition (from λεπίς, lepis, "scale," as in fish scales) rather than specifically Hansen's disease; Hansen's disease was known to ancient Greek medicine as ἐλέφας/ἐλεφαντίασις (elephas/elephantiasis) — a completely different term; the LXX's use of lepra for tzara'at is thus a reasonable translation choice within the Greek medical vocabulary of the 3rd–2nd century BCE, even if later readers have incorrectly equated it with Hansen's disease] — he shall be brought [ve-huva] [ve-huva — "and he shall be brought / and they shall bring him"; niphal perfect of bo', "to come/bring," in the passive: "shall be brought"; the passive phrasing implies that others bring the afflicted person — the person does not simply present himself; there is a communal responsibility to bring the suspected case before the priest; the LXX renders ve-huva as καὶ ἀχθήσεται (kai achthēsetai, "and he shall be led/brought"), using ἄγω (agō, "to lead, to bring")] to Aharon the priest [el-Aharon ha-kohen] [el-Aharon ha-kohen — "to Aharon the priest"; the naming of Aharon specifically (rather than "the priest" generically) underscores that the tzara'at examination is an established priestly institution, here being formally legislated for the first time with Aharon as its inaugural practitioner; the LXX reads πρὸς Ααρων τὸν ἱερέα (pros Aarōn ton hierea, "to Aaron the priest")] or to one of his sons the priests [o el-achad mi-banav ha-kohanim] [o el-achad mi-banav ha-kohanim — "or to one from his sons, the priests"; this extension from Aharon to his sons is a standard formula in Leviticus establishing that the priestly function is hereditary and delegable within the Aaronide line; the legislation applies to the institution of the priesthood, not solely to Aharon as an individual; LXX: ἢ πρὸς ἕνα τῶν υἱῶν αὐτοῦ τῶν ἱερέων (ē pros hena tōn hyiōn autou tōn hiereōn, "or to one of his sons, the priests")].

**3** "And the priest shall examine [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen — "and the priest shall see / and the priest shall examine"; ra'ah ("to see") is used throughout this chapter as the diagnostic technical term: the priest "sees" — visually inspects — the lesion; the priest is the diagnostician-by-sight; no physical probing, blood testing, or instrument is mentioned; all diagnosis is by visual examination; LXX: καὶ ὄψεται ὁ ἱερεύς (kai opsetai ho hiereus, "and the priest shall see")] the mark [et-ha-nega] on the skin of his flesh [be-'or basar] — and the hair in the mark [ve-se'ar ba-nega] has turned white [hafakh lavan] [hafakh lavan — "has been turned white / has changed to white"; hafakh ("to turn, to overturn, to transform") + lavan ("white"); the whitening of the hair within the lesion is a key positive diagnostic criterion; it indicates a disruption of normal skin-and-hair physiology — in modern terms, possibly associated with melanocyte destruction; LXX: καὶ ἡ θρίξ ἐν τῇ ἁφῇ μετέβαλεν λευκή (kai hē thrix en tē haphē metebalon leukē, "and the hair in the mark has turned white") — precise agreement with the MT] and the appearance of the mark [ve-mar'eh ha-nega] is deeper than [amok me] the skin of his flesh [min-'or basar] [amok me-'or basar — "deeper than the skin of his flesh"; amok is from 'amaq ("to be deep"), used here in comparative construction: the lesion appears sunken below the surface level of the surrounding skin; the appearance of depth (not the raised se'et of v.2, but a depression) is the second positive criterion; LXX: καὶ ἡ ὄψις τῆς ἁφῆς ταπεινοτέρα τοῦ δέρματος τοῦ χρωτός αὐτοῦ (kai hē opsis tēs haphēs tapeinotera tou dermatos tou chrōtos autou, "and the appearance of the mark is lower/deeper than the skin of his body"), using ταπεινός (tapeinos, "low, humble, deep") — the humility-word is appropriated for the clinical "sunken" appearance] — it [is] a mark of tzara'at [nega tzara'at hu'] [nega tzara'at hu' — "it is a mark of tzara'at"; hu' is the 3rd masculine singular pronoun used as a copula ("it is") in nominal sentences; the tripartite verdict formula follows: examination (ra'ah) → assessment → verdict (tameh or tahor); LXX: ἁφὴ λέπρας ἐστίν (haphē lepras estin, "it is a touch/mark of leprosy")]; and the priest shall examine him [ve-ra'ahu ha-kohen] and declare him unclean [ve-timme'o] [ve-timme'o — "and he shall declare him unclean / and he shall make him impure"; piel of tame', "to be unclean"; in the piel the verb is declarative-causative: "to pronounce unclean, to declare as being in a state of tum'ah"; the priest does not cause the impurity — he recognizes and officially declares it; this is the formal verdict: tameh; LXX: καὶ ὄψεται αὐτὸν ὁ ἱερεὺς καὶ ἀκαθαρεῖ αὐτόν (kai opsetai auton ho hiereus kai akatherei auton, "and the priest shall see him and shall declare him unclean") — using ἀκαθαρέω (akathareō, "to declare/pronounce unclean"), the denominative verb from ἀκάθαρτος].

**4** "And if the bright-spot [baheret] is white [levana] [levana — the feminine form of lavan, "white"; baheret is feminine in Hebrew, so the adjective agrees in gender: baheret levana, "a white bright-spot"] on the skin of his flesh [be-'or basar] and its appearance [ve-mar'eha] is not deeper than the skin [lo' amok me-ha-'or] and its hair [use'arah] has not turned white [lo' hafakh lavan] — the priest shall shut up [ve-hisgir ha-kohen] [ve-hisgir ha-kohen — "and the priest shall shut up / and the priest shall isolate"; hisgir is hiphil of sagar ("to close, to shut, to lock"), used consistently in this chapter for the quarantine procedure; the priest "closes/shuts" the patient — sequesters them in isolation for a defined period; this is the earliest recorded isolation/quarantine protocol in biblical law; LXX: καὶ συγκλείσει ὁ ἱερεύς (kai synkleisei ho hiereus, "and the priest shall shut up/confine"), using συγκλείω (synkleiō, "to shut up, to enclose, to confine")] the mark [et-ha-nega] seven days [shivat yamim]:

**5** "And the priest shall examine him [ve-ra'ahu ha-kohen] on the seventh day [ba-yom ha-shevi'i] [ba-yom ha-shevi'i — "on the seventh day"; the heptadic structure of the diagnostic examination — 7-day quarantine, re-examination, possible second 7-day quarantine, second re-examination — mirrors the heptadic structures of the creation narrative and the purity legislation of chs.11–12; seven days is the fundamental unit of ritual time in the Levitical system]; and behold [ve-hineh] [ve-hineh — "and look! / and behold!"; hineh is the demonstrative particle drawing attention to what follows — the outcome of the examination; it is used throughout this chapter to introduce the priest's visual finding] the mark [ha-nega] has remained as-it-was [amad be-'eynav] [amad be-'eynav — literally "has stood in his eyes / has remained in his sight"; be-'eynav = "in his eyes"; the phrase amad be-'eynav = "it has held its position as far as he can see / it has not changed in appearance"; this is an important phenomenological criterion: stability of the lesion; LXX: ἡ ἁφὴ μένῃ ἐναντίον αὐτοῦ (hē haphē menē enantion autou, "the mark remains before him"), using μένω (menō, "to remain, to stay")] — the mark has not spread [lo' pasah ha-nega] [lo' pasah ha-nega — "the mark has not spread"; pasah (from a root meaning "to spread out, to expand") is the key criterion of spreading: a spreading lesion is more ominous than a stable one; LXX: ἡ ἁφὴ οὐ μετεπέσεν (hē haphē ou metepesan, "the mark has not changed/spread"), using μεταπίπτω (metapiptō, "to fall into a different state, to change, to shift") — a somewhat looser rendering than the MT's specific pasah ("spread")] on the skin [ba-'or] — the priest shall shut him up [ve-hisgir ha-kohen oto] a second time [sheni'it] [sheni'it — "a second [time]"; the numeral sheni ("second") used adverbially; LXX: ἐπὶ ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας τὸ δεύτερον (epi hepta hēmeras to deuteron, "for seven days a second time")] [another] seven days [shivat yamim]:

**6** "And the priest shall examine him [ve-ra'ah oto ha-kohen] on the seventh day [ba-yom ha-shevi'i] a second time [shenit] [shenit — same as sheni'it in v.5; variant spelling in the MT; LXX: ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἑβδόμῃ ἐκ δευτέρου (en tē hēmera tē hebdomē ek deuterou, "on the seventh day for a second time")] — and behold the mark has dimmed [ve-hineh kehah ha-nega] [kehah ha-nega — "the mark has grown dim / has faded"; kehah is from kahah ("to be faint, to grow dim, to be weak, to fade"); the mark has faded in color intensity — not spreading, not deepening, and now losing its vivid appearance; this is a sign of resolution; LXX: καὶ ἰδοὺ ἡ ἁφὴ ἀμαυρά (kai idou hē haphē amaura, "and behold the mark is dim"), using ἀμαυρός (amauros, "dim, faint, obscure") — a good equivalent for kehah] and the mark has not spread on the skin [ve-lo' pasah ha-nega ba-'or] — the priest shall declare him clean [ve-tihar oto ha-kohen] [ve-tihar oto ha-kohen — "and the priest shall declare him clean"; tihar piel: "to pronounce clean, to declare tahor"; parallel to the declarative-causative timme'o of v.3; the priest pronounces the verdict in both directions — he can declare tameh or tahor; LXX: καὶ καθαριεῖ αὐτὸν ὁ ἱερεύς (kai katharierei auton ho hiereus, "and the priest shall purify him"), using καθαρίζω (katharizō, "to cleanse, to purify")]; it [is] a rash [mispeachat hi'] [mispeachat hi' — "it is a rash/scab"; mispeachat is related to sapachat of v.2 — the same root, perhaps a variant form or a specific sub-type; the faded, non-spreading lesion is now reclassified not as tzara'at but as a mispeachat — a benign rash/scab; LXX: σημασία ἐστίν (sēmasia estin, "it is a marking / an insignificant mark"), from σημαίνω (sēmainō, "to signify, to mark") — a somewhat vaguer rendering]; and he shall wash his clothes [ve-kibes begadav] [ve-kibes begadav — "and he shall wash his garments"; kibbes is piel of kabas ("to wash, to trample, to launder") — the intensive-action washing of garments, as contrasted with the lighter rachats ("to bathe/rinse"); kibes appears throughout the purity legislation for the thorough laundering required as part of ritual purification; LXX: καὶ πλυνεῖ τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ (kai plynei ta himatia autou, "and he shall wash his garments"), using πλύνω (plynō, "to wash clothes") — the standard LXX equivalent of kibes] and he shall be clean [ve-taher] [ve-taher — "and he shall be clean"; qal perfect with consecutive-vav: the washing of clothes is followed by the restored state of ritual purity; LXX: καὶ καθαρὸς ἔσται (kai katharos estai, "and he shall be clean")].

**7** "But if the rash [ha-mispeachat] spreads widely [pasoh tifshah] [pasoh tifshah — "spreading it shall spread"; the infinitive absolute of pasah (pasoh) before the finite verb (tifshah) gives emphasis and certainty: it has spread, undeniably spread; the absolute infinitive construction in Hebrew functions as an intensive: "spread indeed / truly has spread"; LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἐξανθήσῃ ἐξανθοῦσα (ean de exanthēsē exanthōsa, "and if blossoming it blossoms"), using ἐξανθέω (exantheō, "to bloom out, to break out in a rash, to blossom/spread") — the LXX's "blooming" metaphor is interesting, using a botanical image for the spread of a skin lesion] on the skin [ba-'or] after [achar] he was shown to the priest [hera'oto el-ha-kohen] for his cleansing [le-taharat'o] [le-taharat'o — "for his purification / for his being declared clean"; taharah ("purity/cleansing") used as the purpose of the priestly presentation; the verse sets up the scenario where an initial clean verdict is reversed by subsequent spreading; LXX: ὃ ἐδείχθη τῷ ἱερεῖ εἰς καθαρισμόν αὐτοῦ (ho edeikhthē tō hierei eis katharismon autou, "that which was shown to the priest for his cleansing")] — he shall be shown again [ve-nir'ah shenit] [ve-nir'ah shenit — "and he shall be seen again a second time"; niphal of ra'ah ("to be seen, to appear, to show oneself") — the afflicted person presents himself before the priest a second time; LXX: ὀφθήσεται πρὸς τὸν ἱερέα (ophthēsetai pros ton hierea, "he shall appear before the priest"), using ὁράω (horaō, "to see") in the passive; note the LXX drops the "a second time" here but has it in v.5] to the priest [el-ha-kohen]:

**8** "And the priest shall examine [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] and behold the rash has spread on the skin [ve-hineh pasah ha-mispeachat ba-'or] — the priest shall declare him unclean [ve-timme'o ha-kohen] [ve-timme'o ha-kohen — "and the priest shall declare him unclean"; piel declarative, as in v.3; the initial clean verdict is reversed by the spreading criterion; LXX: ἀκαθαρεῖ αὐτὸν ὁ ἱερεύς (akatharierei auton ho hiereus, "the priest shall declare him unclean")]; it [is] tzara'at [tzara'at hi'] [tzara'at hi' — "it is tzara'at"; the bare nominal sentence: the reclassification from mispeachat (benign rash) to tzara'at (serious condition requiring declaration of tameh); LXX: λέπρα ἐστίν (lepra estin, "it is leprosy")].

---

**9** "A mark of tzara'at [nega tzara'at] [nega tzara'at — "a mark/affliction of tzara'at"; the same opening phrase as in v.2, beginning the second diagnostic unit (vv.9–17): the chronic or advanced case; unlike vv.1–8 where the diagnosis is uncertain and requires a waiting period, vv.9–17 deal with an established, visible case; LXX: ἁφὴ λέπρας (haphē lepras, "a touch/mark of leprosy")] when [ki] it is in a man [ki tihyeh be-adam] [ki tihyeh be-adam — "when it is in a man / when it comes to be in a human being"; the same adam ("human being") as v.2 — the laws apply to all persons; LXX: ἐὰν γένηται ἐν ἀνθρώπῳ (ean genētai en anthrōpō, "if it comes to be in a man")] — he shall be brought [ve-huva] to the priest [el-ha-kohen]:

**10** "And the priest shall examine [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] and behold [ve-hineh] a white swelling [se'et levana] in the skin [ba-'or] and it has turned [hafakh] the hair white [se'ar lavan] and raw flesh [u-michyat basar chai] [u-michyat basar chai — "and a living/raw flesh of life"; michyah is from chayah ("to live"), meaning "living tissue / raw flesh / a place of live tissue"; basar chai = "living flesh / raw flesh" — unhealed, exposed, raw tissue; the combination michyat basar chai = "the living rawness of the flesh" — a patch of exposed, raw, living flesh within or around the lesion; this is a major ominous criterion; LXX: ὁ χρὼς ὁ ζῶν (ho chrōs ho zōn, "the living skin"), using ζάω (zaō, "to live") for chai — precise equivalent] in the swelling [ba-se'et]:

**11** "A chronic tzara'at [tzara'at noshenet] [tzara'at noshenet — "an old tzara'at / a long-standing tzara'at"; noshenet is qal participle of yashan ("to be old, to be of long duration, to become stale"); the feminine participial form agrees with tzara'at (feminine noun); noshenet describes a tzara'at that has become chronic — established over time; this is not a new or ambiguous lesion but an advanced, persistent case; LXX: λέπρα παλαιά (lepra palaia, "an old leprosy"), using παλαιός (palaios, "old, ancient, of long standing") — precise equivalent] it is in the skin of his flesh [hi' be-'or basar] — and the priest shall declare him unclean [ve-timme'enu ha-kohen] [ve-timme'enu ha-kohen — "and the priest shall declare him unclean"; the suffix -enu = "him"; note the direct verdict without quarantine — the advanced case requires no waiting period; the criteria are conclusive; LXX: ἀκαθαρεῖ αὐτὸν ὁ ἱερεύς (akatharierei auton ho hiereus)]; he shall not shut him up [lo' yasgirenu] [lo' yasgirenu — "he shall not shut him up / he shall not quarantine him"; the negative of hisgir; in this advanced/chronic case, the quarantine period is unnecessary — the diagnosis is already unambiguous; the quarantine served the purpose of monitoring uncertain cases; a clearly advanced case does not require monitoring; LXX: καὶ οὐκ ἀποκλείσει αὐτόν (kai ouk apokleisei auton, "and he shall not shut him up"), using ἀποκλείω (apokleiō, "to shut off, to exclude")] for he [is] unclean [tameh hu'] [tameh hu' — "he is unclean"; the declarative nominal sentence; LXX: ὅτι ἀκάθαρτός ἐστιν (hoti akathartos estin, "for he is unclean")].

**12** "And if the tzara'at breaks out wildly [paroach tifrach] [paroach tifrach — "breaking out it shall break out"; another infinitive-absolute construction expressing intensity and completeness: the tzara'at "breaking out breaks out" — erupts all over; paroach and tifrach are both from parach ("to break out, to blossom, to burst forth") — the same botanical "blossoming" imagery used in the LXX at v.7; LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἐξανθοῦσα ἐξανθήσῃ (ean de exanthousa exanthēsē, "if blossoming it blossoms")] on the skin [ba-'or] and the tzara'at covers all the skin of the mark [ve-khissah ha-tzara'at et kol-'or ha-nega] from his head to his feet [me-rosho ve-ad-raglav] [me-rosho ve-ad-raglav — "from his head to his feet"; this phrase covers the entire body surface — a total-body eruption; LXX: κατακαλύψῃ τὴν λέπραν ὅλον τὸ δέρμα τῆς ἁφῆς ἀπὸ κεφαλῆς ἕως ποδῶν (katakaly psē tēn lepran holon to derma tēs haphēs apo kephalēs heōs podōn, "covers the leprosy the whole skin of the mark from head to feet")], wherever [be-khol] the priest's eye can see [mar'eh ha-kohen] [be-khol mar'eh 'eynei ha-kohen — "in all the sight of the eyes of the priest"; the full phrase reads be-khol mar'eh 'eynei ha-kohen, "in all the seeing of the priest's eyes" — in every place that the priest can visually examine]:

**13** "And the priest shall examine [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] and behold [ve-hineh] the tzara'at has covered all his flesh [kissatah ha-tzara'at et kol-besaro] — he shall declare clean [ve-tihar] [ve-tihar — "and he shall declare clean"; the counterintuitive verdict: total-body tzara'at (all-white) = clean; partial tzara'at (with raw flesh) = unclean; the logical basis is that when the eruption covers the entire body and all the skin has turned white and uniform, there is no longer a visible nega — no local lesion distinct from the surrounding skin; the person has passed through the condition and the skin is uniformly (if abnormally) the same; some interpreters connect this to conditions like total vitiligo; the LXX renders ve-tihar as καθαριεῖ (kathariei, "he shall declare clean")] the mark [et-ha-nega] — all of it [kulo] has turned white [hafakh lavan] — he is clean [tahor hu'] [tahor hu' — "he is clean"; the terse nominal sentence of the tahor verdict]:

**14** "But on the day [u-ve-yom] raw flesh [basar chai] appears in him [hera'ot bo] — he shall be unclean [ve-tame] [ve-tame — "and he shall be unclean"; here qal perfect with consecutive-vav: "and he is/shall be unclean"; the re-appearance of raw flesh reverses the all-white-clean verdict; raw flesh within a white lesion is always an ominous sign; LXX: τῇ δὲ ἡμέρᾳ ὀφθῇ ἐν αὐτῷ χρὼς ζῶν, ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται (tē de hēmera ophthē en autō chrōs zōn, akathartos estai, "on the day living skin appears in him, he shall be unclean")]:

**15** "And the priest shall examine [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] the raw flesh [et-basar ha-chai] and declare him unclean [ve-timme'o] [ve-timme'o — declarative piel, as above] — the raw flesh [basar ha-chai] is unclean [tameh hu'] [tameh hu'] — it [is] tzara'at [tzara'at hi'] [tzara'at hi']:

**16** "Or if the raw flesh [o ki-yashuv basar ha-chai] turns [yashuv] and changes [ve-nehpakh] to white [le-lavan] [o ki-yashuv basar ha-chai ve-nehpakh le-lavan — "or if the raw flesh returns and turns to white"; yashuv is "it returns / turns back"; nehpakh is niphal of hafakh ("to turn, to overturn"), in the passive: "is turned, becomes"; the raw flesh resolving into white skin — whitening over — is a sign of healing/improvement; LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἀποστρέψῃ ὁ χρὼς ὁ ζῶν καὶ μεταβάλῃ εἰς λευκόν (ean de apostrepsē ho chrōs ho zōn kai metabalē eis leukon, "if the living skin turns back and changes to white")] — he shall come [u-va] to the priest [el-ha-kohen]:

**17** "And the priest shall examine him [ve-ra'ahu ha-kohen] and behold [ve-hineh] the mark has turned white [hafakh ha-nega le-lavan] — the priest shall declare the mark clean [ve-tihar ha-kohen et-ha-nega] [ve-tihar ha-kohen et-ha-nega — "and the priest shall declare the mark clean"; the direct-object marker et precedes ha-nega ("the mark"), indicating it is the mark, not the person per se, that is declared clean; the priest pronounces on the nega, and the person is clean by extension; LXX: καθαριεῖ ὁ ἱερεὺς τὴν ἁφήν (kathariei ho hiereus tēn haphēn, "the priest shall declare the mark clean")] — he is clean [tahor hu'] [tahor hu'].

---

**18** "And flesh [ve-vasar] when [ki] there is in it [yihyeh vo] in its skin [be-oro] a boil [shechim] [shechim — [boil/ulcer]; shechim is from a root meaning "to sink, to be depressed"; it refers to a sunken lesion — a boil, ulcer, or abscess that has left a depression in the skin; this begins the third diagnostic unit (vv.18–23): tzara'at-like conditions arising from a healed boil; LXX: ἕλκος (helkos, "ulcer, sore, wound") — a reasonable equivalent, though helkos is broader than shechim] and it has healed [ve-nirpa] [ve-nirpa — "and it has been healed"; niphal of rapa' ("to heal"), passive: "and it was healed / has healed"; the condition discussed in vv.18–23 begins with a pre-existing healed boil — the lesion arises from the site of a former shechim; LXX: καὶ ἰαθῇ (kai iathē, "and it has been healed"), using ἰάομαι (iaomai, "to heal") — the standard LXX healing verb]:

**19** "And there arises [ve-qam] in the place of the boil [bi-mqom ha-shechim] a white swelling [se'et levana] or a bright reddish-white spot [baheret levana admedenet] [baheret levana admedenet — "a bright spot white-reddish"; baheret + levana ("white") + admedenet ("reddish"); admedenet is from adam ("red") in the denominative / adjectival form, meaning "tending toward red / reddish"; the compound baheret levana admedenet is thus "a white spot tending to redness" — a pale lesion with a reddish tinge; this is the only occurrence in the chapter of this compound two-color descriptor; LXX: τηλαύγημα λευκὸν ὑπωχρόν (tēlaugēma leukon hypōchron, "a bright spot white and pale"), using ὑπωχρός (hypōchros, "slightly pale, pallid") — which drops the reddish quality and substitutes pallor; this is a minor LXX divergence from the MT's "reddish"] — he shall be shown to the priest [ve-nir'ah el-ha-kohen]:

**20** "And the priest shall examine [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] and behold [ve-hineh] its appearance [mar'eha] is deeper than the skin [shafal me-ha-'or] [shafal me-ha-'or — "is low/sunken below the skin"; shafal is from shafal ("to be low, to be humble, to be sunk"); parallel to amok of v.3; LXX: ταπεινοτέρα τοῦ δέρματος (tapeinotera tou dermatos, "lower than the skin") — same rendering as v.3] and its hair [se'arah] has turned white [hafakh lavan] — the priest shall declare him unclean [ve-timme'enu ha-kohen] [ve-timme'enu ha-kohen] — it [is] a mark of tzara'at [nega tzara'at hi'] [nega tzara'at hi'] that has broken out [parachah] [parachah — "has broken out / has blossomed"; qal perfect 3fs of parach; the boil-site has "blossomed" into tzara'at; LXX: ἐν τῷ ἕλκει ἐξήνθηκεν (en tō helkei exēnthēken, "it has broken out in the ulcer")] in the boil [ba-shechim]:

**21** "But if the priest examines it [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen oto] and behold [ve-hineh] there is no white hair in it [ein bo se'ar lavan] and it is not deeper than the skin [ve-lo' shafal hu' min-ha-'or] and it is dim [ve-hu' keheh] [ve-hu' keheh — "and it is faded/dim"; keheh as in v.6 — the faded, less-vivid appearance; LXX: καὶ αὐτὸ ἀμαυρόν (kai auto amauron, "and it is dim")] — the priest shall shut him up [ve-hisgir ha-kohen oto] seven days [shivat yamim]:

**22** "And if it spreads widely [ufarsoh tifsah] [ufarsoh tifsah — "and spreading it spreads"; another infinitive-absolute emphatic; parah/parash here in the context of spreading; MT has pisah/pashah in other verses; LXX: ἐὰν δὲ διαχεόμενον διαχυθῇ (ean de diacheomenon diachythē, "if spreading it spreads"), using διαχέω (diacheō, "to pour out, to spread, to diffuse")] on the skin [ba-'or] — the priest shall declare him unclean [ve-timme'o ha-kohen] [ve-timme'o ha-kohen] — it [is] a mark [nega hu'] [nega hu']:

**23** "But if the bright-spot [ha-baheret] stays in its place [ba-meqomah taamod] [ba-meqomah ta'amod — "in its place it stands / it stands still in its place"; the idiom of stability, as in v.5 (amad be-'eynav); a stable lesion = better prognosis; LXX: ἐὰν δὲ κατὰ χώραν μείνῃ τὸ τηλαύγημα (ean de kata chōran meinē to tēlaugēma, "if the bright spot remains in its place"), using μένω (menō, "to remain")], it has not spread [lo' fasah] — it [is] a scar of the boil [tsarevel ha-shechim hi'] [tsarevel ha-shechim hi' — "it is the scar/blister of the boil"; tsarevel occurs only here and at v.28 in the Hebrew Bible; from a root possibly meaning "to burn, to scar"; it refers to the scar or remnant of a healed boil — cicatricial tissue; LXX: οὐλὴ τοῦ ἕλκους ἐστίν (oulē tou helkous estin, "it is a scar of the ulcer"), using οὐλή (oulē, "scar") — a reasonable equivalent] and the priest shall declare him clean [ve-tihar oto ha-kohen] [ve-tihar oto ha-kohen] [tahor hu' — "he is clean"].

---

**24** "Or flesh [o vasar] when [ki] there is [yihyeh] in its skin [be-oro] a burn from fire [mikve'at-esh] [mikve'at-esh — [burn from fire]; mikve'at is from kava' ("to burn, to brand, to scorch"), giving mikve'ah ("a burning, a brand, a burn wound"); mikve'at-esh = "a burning of fire / a fire-burn"; the fourth diagnostic unit (vv.24–28) deals with tzara'at-like lesions arising from a burn scar; LXX: κατάκαυμα πυρός (katakayma pyros, "a burning of fire"), using κατάκαυμα (katakayma, "a burning, a burn wound") — a precise compound] and the raw flesh of the burn [michyat ha-mikve'ah] becomes [ve-hayah] a bright reddish-white spot [baheret levana admedenet] or white [o levana] [o levana — "or white"; the baheret of a burn site may be baheret levana admedenet ("reddish-white") or simply levana ("white"); two presentations are possible]:

**25** "And the priest shall examine it [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] and behold [ve-hineh] the hair in the bright-spot [ha-se'ar ba-baheret] has turned white [hafakh lavan] and its appearance [ve-mar'eha] is deeper than the skin [amok min-ha-'or] — it [is] tzara'at [tzara'at hi'] [tzara'at hi'] that has broken out in the burn [ba-mikve'ah parachah] [ba-mikve'ah parachah — "in the burn it has broken out"; LXX: ἐν τῷ κατακαύματι ἐξήνθηκεν (en tō katakaumati exēnthēken, "in the burning it has broken out")] — and the priest shall declare him unclean [ve-timme'enu ha-kohen] [ve-timme'enu ha-kohen] — it [is] a mark of tzara'at [nega tzara'at hi'] [nega tzara'at hi']:

**26** "But if the priest examines it [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] and behold [ve-hineh] there is no white hair in the bright-spot [be-ha-baheret] and it is not deeper [ve-lo' shafal] than the skin and it is dim [ve-hu' keheh] [ve-hu' keheh] — the priest shall shut him up [ve-hisgir oto ha-kohen] seven days [shivat yamim]:

**27** "And the priest shall examine him [ve-ra'ahu ha-kohen] on the seventh day [ba-yom ha-shevi'i] — if it spreads widely [im-pasoh tifsah] [im-pasoh tifsah — "if spreading it spreads"; the infinitive absolute again; LXX: ἐὰν μεγαλυνθῇ μεγαλυνθῇ (ean megalynthē megalynthē, "if it enlarges / if it enlarges") — the LXX uses μεγαλύνω (megalynō, "to enlarge, to increase") twice, rendering the infinitive-absolute construction by repetition of the same word, a natural Greek equivalent of the Hebrew emphasis technique] on the skin [ba-'or] — the priest shall declare him unclean [ve-timme'o ha-kohen] [ve-timme'o ha-kohen] — it [is] a mark of tzara'at [nega tzara'at hi'] [nega tzara'at hi']:

**28** "But if the bright-spot [ha-baheret] stays in its place [ba-meqomah ta'amod] [ba-meqomah ta'amod] and has not spread [lo' fasah] on the skin [ba-'or] and it is dim [ve-hi' kehah] [ve-hi' kehah — "and it is faded/dim"; hi' ("it/she") is feminine, agreeing with baheret; LXX: καὶ αὐτὸ ἀμαυρόν (kai auto amauron, "and it is dim")] — it [is] a swelling of the burn [se'et ha-mikve'ah hi'] [se'et ha-mikve'ah hi' — "it is the swelling of the burn"; the benign residual swelling/scar from the burn, parallel to the tsarevel ha-shechim ("scar of the boil") of v.23; LXX: φλέγμα καύματός ἐστιν (phlegma kaumatios estin, "it is the inflammation/swelling of the burning"), using φλέγμα (phlegma, "inflammation, swelling, phlegm") — an interesting choice; in ancient Greek medicine phlegma was one of the four humors, associated with moisture and pallor] — and the priest shall declare him clean [ve-tihar oto ha-kohen] [ve-tihar oto ha-kohen] for it [is] a scar of the burn [tsarevel ha-mikve'ah hi'] [tsarevel ha-mikve'ah hi' — the same tsarevel ("scar") as in v.23, now applied to the burn-scar; LXX: οὐλὴ καύματός ἐστιν (oulē kaumatos estin, "it is a scar of the burning")].

---

**29** "And a man [ish] or a woman [o ishah] — when [ki] there is in them [yihyeh vo] a mark [nega] on the head [be-rosh] or on the beard [o ba-zaqan] [o ba-zaqan — "or on the beard"; zaqan is "beard" — specifically the beard area, the chin and cheek region where facial hair grows; this fifth diagnostic unit (vv.29–37) addresses tzara'at-like lesions of the head and beard; the inclusion of both men (who have beards) and women (who have head hair) in the same unit, with the specific mention of the beard area alongside the head, reflects that the neteq (v.30) can occur in the scalp or in the beard region; LXX: ἐν αὐτῷ ἐν τῇ κεφαλῇ ἢ ἐν τῷ πώγωνι (en autō en tē kephalē ē en tō pōgōni, "in him on the head or on the beard"), using πώγων (pōgōn, "beard")]:

**30** "And the priest shall examine [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] the mark [et-ha-nega] and behold [ve-hineh] its appearance [mar'ehu] is deeper than the skin [amok min-ha-'or] and in it [vo] fine yellow hair [se'ar tzahov dak] [se'ar tzahov dak — "hair yellow thin"; tzahov is from tsahav or tsahab ("to be yellow, to glisten"), a yellowish or golden color; dak is "thin, fine, delicate"; se'ar tzahov dak = "fine yellow hair"; in the context of scalp/beard lesions, the yellowing and thinning of hair within the lesion is the key diagnostic criterion — as opposed to the whitening of hair that signals tzara'at in body lesions (cf. vv.3, 20, 25); the different color criterion for head/beard lesions (yellow rather than white) may reflect the different appearance of hair conditions in scalp/beard — the yellowing and breaking of hair in scalp conditions may correspond to modern tinea capitis (ringworm of the scalp) or other fungal scalp infections; LXX: ἐν αὐτῷ τρίχα ξανθίζουσαν λεπτήν (en autō tricha xanthi zousan leptēn, "in it yellowing thin hair"), using ξανθίζω (xanthizō, "to make yellow, to become tawny/golden") and λεπτός (leptos, "thin, fine, slender")] — the priest shall declare him unclean [ve-timme'o ha-kohen] [ve-timme'o ha-kohen] — it [is] a neteq [neteq hu'] [neteq hu' — "it is a neteq"; neteq [נֶתֶק] is the technical term for the head/beard tzara'at condition, used only in this passage; from nataq ("to pull out, to tear away"), it suggests a tearing-away of hair; LXX: θέρισμα ἀκάθαρτον ἐστίν (therisma akatharton estin, "it is an unclean crop/harvest"), using θέρισμα (therisma, "a harvesting, a crop, a cutting") — possibly understanding nateq as "a pulling-out/cutting" of hair; alternatively LXX may read neteq as related to nataph ("to drop, to fall") in the sense of falling/thinning hair], a tzara'at [tzara'at] of the head [ha-rosh] or of the beard [o ha-zaqan] [o ha-zaqan; LXX: λέπρα τῆς κεφαλῆς ἢ τοῦ πώγωνός ἐστιν (lepra tēs kephalēs ē tou pōgōnos estin, "it is a leprosy of the head or of the beard")] it [is]:

**31** "And if the priest examines [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] the mark of the neteq [et-nega ha-neteq] and behold [ve-hineh] its appearance [mar'ehu] is not deeper than the skin [lo' amok min-ha-'or] and there is no black hair [ve-se'ar shachor] in it [eyn bo] [ve-se'ar shachor eyn bo — "and black hair is not in it"; shachor = "black"; the absence of black hair is the criterion here — normal scalp/beard hair being black (or dark) in Middle Eastern populations; the absence of black hair within the lesion area indicates the hair has either fallen out or been replaced by the thin yellow hair of v.30; LXX: καὶ τρίξ μέλαινα οὐκ ἔστιν ἐν αὐτῷ (kai thrix melaina ouk estin en autō, "and black hair is not in it"), using μέλας (melas, "black")] — the priest shall shut up [ve-hisgir ha-kohen] the mark of the neteq [et-nega ha-neteq] seven days [shivat yamim]:

**32** "And the priest shall examine [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] the mark [et-ha-nega] on the seventh day [ba-yom ha-shevi'i] and behold [ve-hineh] the neteq has not spread [lo' fasah ha-neteq] and there is no yellow hair in it [ve-se'ar tzahov eyn bo] and the appearance of the neteq [ve-mar'eh ha-neteq] is not deeper than the skin [lo' amok min-ha-'or]:

**33** "Then he shall shave himself [ve-hitgalach] [ve-hitgalach — "and he shall shave himself"; hitpael of galach ("to shave, to be shaved"), the reflexive form: "he shall have himself shaved / shall shave himself"; the shaving of the area around the neteq is the prescribed procedure during the quarantine period; importantly, the neteq-area itself is not shaved (ve-et-ha-neteq lo yegalach — "but the neteq he shall not shave"), preserving the diagnostic evidence within the lesion; LXX: καὶ ξυρηθήσεται (kai xyrēthēsetai, "and he shall be shaved"), using ξυράω (xyrāō, "to shave") in the passive — the LXX makes this a passive rather than reflexive, which may imply a barber does the shaving] — but the neteq [ve-et-ha-neteq] he shall not shave [lo' yegalach] [lo' yegalach — "he shall not shave"; the diagnostic area must be preserved unshaved so the hair-color evidence remains visible for the second examination; LXX: τὸ δὲ θέρισμα οὐ ξυρήσει (to de therisma ou xyrēsei, "but the harvest/crop he shall not shave")] — and the priest shall shut up [ve-hisgir ha-kohen] the neteq [et-ha-neteq] a second time [sheni'it] [sheni'it] seven days [shivat yamim]:

**34** "And the priest shall examine [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] the neteq [et-ha-neteq] on the seventh day [ba-yom ha-shevi'i] — and behold [ve-hineh] the neteq has not spread [lo' fasah ha-neteq] on the skin [ba-'or] and its appearance [ve-mar'ehu] is not deeper than the skin [lo' amok min-ha-'or] — the priest shall declare him clean [ve-tihar oto ha-kohen] [ve-tihar oto ha-kohen] and he shall wash his clothes [ve-kibes begadav] and be clean [ve-taher] [ve-taher]:

**35** "But if the neteq [ve-im-pasoh yifsah ha-neteq] spreads widely [pasoh yifsah] [pasoh yifsah — "spreading it will spread"; the infinitive-absolute construction of spreading, now applied to the neteq in the post-clean period; LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ἐξανθοῦν ἐξανθήσῃ τὸ θέρισμα (ean de exanthoyn exanthēsē to therisma)] on the skin [ba-'or] after his purification [achar taharat'o] [achar taharat'o — "after his cleansing / after his being declared clean"]:

**36** "And the priest shall examine him [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen oto] and behold [ve-hineh] the neteq has spread [pasah ha-neteq] on the skin [ba-'or] — the priest shall not look for the yellow hair [lo' yevaqer ha-kohen la-se'ar ha-tzahov] [lo' yevaqer ha-kohen la-se'ar ha-tzahov — "the priest shall not search for the yellow hair"; yevaqer is piel of baqar ("to seek, to examine, to investigate diligently"); in the case of confirmed spreading, the secondary yellow-hair criterion becomes irrelevant — the spread alone is decisive; the priest need not perform the additional examination; LXX: καὶ οὐκ ἐπισκέψεται ὁ ἱερεύς περὶ τῆς τριχὸς τῆς ξανθῆς (kai ouk episkepsetai ho hiereus peri tēs trichos tēs xanthēs, "and the priest shall not examine regarding the yellow hair"), using ἐπισκέπτομαι (episkeptomai, "to visit, to examine, to inspect")] — he is unclean [tameh hu'] [tameh hu']:

**37** "But if in his eyes [ve-im be-'eynav] the neteq has held still [amad ha-neteq] and black hair [ve-se'ar shachor] has grown in it [tzamach bo] [tzamach bo — "has sprouted in it / has grown in it"; tzamach is from tsamach ("to sprout, to grow, to bud") — the same word used of plants sprouting (Gen 2:5; 3:18); the regrowth of black hair within the neteq is the sign of healing — the normal pigmented hair has returned, indicating resolution of the condition; LXX: εἰ δὲ ἐν τοῖς ὀφθαλμοῖς αὐτοῦ ἕστηκεν τὸ θέρισμα καὶ τρίξ μέλαινα ἀνέτειλεν ἐν αὐτῷ (ei de en tois ophthalmois autou hestēken to therisma kai thrix melaina aneteilen en autō, "if in his eyes the harvest/crop has stood still and black hair has risen up in it"), using ἀνατέλλω (anatellō, "to rise up, to spring forth") — the same word used of the rising of stars and the sun; the sun-rising / sprouting metaphor for hair regrowth is evocative] — the neteq has healed [nirpa ha-neteq] [nirpa ha-neteq — "the neteq has healed"; niphal of rapa', "to be healed, to be cured" — the passive-intransitive: the condition has healed of itself; LXX: ἴαται τὸ θέρισμα (iatai to therisma, "the harvest/crop has been healed"), using ἰάομαι (iaomai, "to heal")]; he is clean [tahor hu'] [tahor hu'] and the priest shall declare him clean [ve-tihar oto ha-kohen] [ve-tihar oto ha-kohen].

---

**38** "And a man [ve-ish] or a woman [o ishah] — when [ki] there are on the skin of their flesh [yihyeh ve-'or basar] bright-spots [beharot] [beharot — the plural of baheret; the sixth diagnostic unit (vv.38–39) addresses multiple bright spots — the benign case; LXX: τηλαυγήματα (tēlaugēmata, "bright marks")], bright-spots of white [beharot levant levant] [beharot levant levant — "bright spots, white white"; the doubling of levant ("white") is a rhetorical repetition for emphasis — very white spots; alternatively parsed as "bright spots of white, white" = uniformly white bright spots; LXX: τηλαυγήματα λαμπρά, λευκά (tēlaugēmata lampra, leuka, "bright marks, brilliant, white"), using two adjectives (λαμπρός, lampros, "brilliant, shining" + λευκός, leukos, "white") to render the Hebrew doubling]:

**39** "And the priest shall examine [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] and behold [ve-hineh] on the skin of their flesh [be-'or basar] [dim] white bright-spots [beharot kehot levant] [beharot kehot levant — "bright spots, faded, white"; kehot is the plural/intensive form of keheh ("faded, dim") — the spots are white but faded/dull, lacking the vivid brightness of an ominous lesion; LXX: τηλαυγήματα ἀμαυρὰ λευκά (tēlaugēmata amaura leuka, "dim white bright marks")] — it [is] bohaq [bohaq hu'] [bohaq hu' — "it is bohaq"; bohaq [בֹּהַק] is a hapax legomenon — it occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible; it is the name of the benign skin condition of v.39 (multiple, dull-white, non-spreading spots), distinguished from the ominous tzara'at of v.38-type lesions; the term may be cognate with Aramaic/Arabic roots meaning "brightness" or "shining"; modern dermatologists have often equated bohaq with vitiligo (depigmented patches), though the text does not specify; LXX: ἀχροίαμα (achroiama, "discoloration, loss of color") — a rare Greek medical term from ἄχροος (achroōs, "without color, pale") — a reasonable equivalent for a benign depigmentation condition], it has broken out [parachah] [parachah — "it has broken out / blossomed"; the same parachah of vv.20, 25, indicating eruption; even the benign bohaq "breaks out" in the same vocabulary as tzara'at — it requires examination because its initial presentation is indistinguishable from the ominous forms] on the skin [ba-'or] — he [is] clean [tahor hu'] [tahor hu'].

---

**40** "And a man [ve-ish] when [ki] his head falls out [yimaret rosho] [yimaret rosho — "his head becomes bald / the hair falls out from his head"; marat is from marats or from the root מרט ("to be stripped bare, to be made smooth"), meaning to become bald; yimaret is niphal imperfect: "his head is made bald"; LXX: ἀνὴρ ᾧ ἐὰν πέσῃ ἡ θρίξ τῆς κεφαλῆς αὐτοῦ (anēr hō ean pesē hē thrix tēs kephalēs autou, "a man from whom the hair of his head falls") — the LXX renders baldness as hair-falling rather than head-being-stripped] — he is [hu'] bald [qereach] [qereach — [bald]; qereach is from qareach ("to be bald"), describing natural/common baldness — the ordinary balding man; LXX: φαλακρός (phalakros, "bald") — the standard Greek term for a bald person (cf. 2 Kgs 2:23 LXX where the boys taunt Elisha as φαλακρέ, "baldy!")]; he is clean [tahor hu'] [tahor hu']:

**41** "And if from the front of his head [ve-im mi-peat rosho] his hair falls out [yimaret] — he [is] bald of forehead [gibbeach] [gibbeach — [bald-forehead]; gibbeach is a different type of baldness from qereach of v.40; the distinction may be between ordinary vertex/crown baldness (qereach) and frontal/forehead-hairline recession (gibbeach); the two terms together in vv.40–44 carefully distinguish the type and location of baldness before assessing whether a tzara'at-type lesion is present; LXX: ἀναφάλαντος (anaphalantos, "bald in front") — a precise compound from ἀνά (ana, "up, back") + φαλακρός (phalakros, "bald")]; he is clean [tahor hu'] [tahor hu']:

**42** "But if [ve-ki] on the bald-head [ba-qarchah] or on the bald-forehead [o ba-gabachah] [ba-qarchah o ba-gabachah — "in the bald-spot or in the forehead-bald-spot"; qarchah and gabachah are the abstract noun forms of qereach and gibbeach, denoting the bald area itself rather than the person; the lesion must be specifically located in the bald area to qualify for this diagnostic unit; LXX: ἐν τῇ φαλάκρᾳ ἢ ἐν τῇ ἀναφαλαντίᾳ (en tē phalakra ē en tē anaphalantia, "in the bald-spot or in the fore-bald-spot")] a white-reddish mark [nega levana admedenet] [nega levana admedenet — "a white-reddish mark"; the same admedenet ("reddish") of v.19, applied now to a lesion on a bald head; the baldness removes the hair-criterion (white/yellow hair), making color and depth the sole diagnostic features; LXX: γένηται ἁφὴ λευκὴ ὑπωχρός (genētai haphē leukē hypōchros, "there comes to be a white pallid mark")] arises [hu' tzara'at poráchat] [hu' tzara'at porachah — "it is tzara'at breaking out / it is a sprouting tzara'at"; porachah from parach, "breaking out, blossoming"; LXX: λέπρα ἀνθοῦσα ἐστίν (lepra anthousa estin, "it is a blooming leprosy"), using ἀνθέω (antheō, "to flower, to bloom")] in his bald-head or his bald-forehead:

**43** "And the priest shall examine him [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] and behold [ve-hineh] the swelling of the mark [se'at ha-nega] [se'at ha-nega — "the swelling/rising of the mark"; se'at is the construct form of se'et ("swelling") — the lesion manifests as a raised, swollen mark on the bald skin] is white-reddish [levana admedenet] on his bald-head or his bald-forehead [be-qarchat'o o ve-gabachato] [be-qarchat'o o ve-gabachato] like the appearance of tzara'at [ke-mar'eh tzara'at] [ke-mar'eh tzara'at — "like the appearance of tzara'at"; the bald-head lesion is compared to tzara'at of the skin in appearance; the priest must recognize the same diagnostic features (raised, white-reddish, characteristic appearance) despite the absence of hair-evidence] of the skin of the flesh [be-'or basar]:

**44** "A tzara'at-afflicted man [ish-metzora] he is [hu'] — unclean he [is] [tameh hu'] [tameh hu'] — the priest shall surely declare him unclean [tameh yetam'enu ha-kohen] [tameh yetam'enu ha-kohen — "unclean, the priest shall declare him unclean"; tameh yetam'enu is another infinitive-absolute construction with the adverbial tameh (absolute: "unclean") intensifying the finite verb yetam'enu (piel imperfect: "shall declare him unclean"): "declaring unclean, the priest shall declare him unclean" — "the priest shall certainly declare him unclean / the priest shall emphatically pronounce him unclean"; LXX: μιανεῖ αὐτὸν ὁ ἱερεύς (mianaiei auton ho hiereus, "the priest shall defile/pronounce him defiled"), using μιαίνω (miainō, "to stain, to defile, to pollute")] — his mark [nega'o] is on his head [be-rosho] [be-rosho]:

---

**45** "And the person who has the tzara'at mark [ve-ha-tzarua' asher bo ha-nega] [ve-ha-tzarua' asher bo ha-nega — "and the one stricken-with-tzara'at in whom is the mark"; ha-tzarua' is the qal passive participle of tzara' ("to be afflicted with tzara'at"), meaning "the tzara'at-afflicted person / the metzora"; this is the first occurrence of tzarua' as a substantive, the technical term for a person who has received the tameh verdict for tzara'at; asher bo ha-nega = "in whom the mark is" — the participial relative clause identifies the condition; LXX: ὁ δὲ λεπρὸς ἐν ᾧ ἐστιν ἡ ἁφή (ho de lepros en hō estin hē haphē, "and the leper in whom the mark is"), using λεπρός (lepros, "leper") — the LXX here introduces the substantive "leper" for the first time, which has profoundly shaped Western usage of the term; critically, this is a ritual/legal designation, not a medical one] — his garments [begadav] shall be torn [yihyu ferumim] [yihyu ferumim — "shall be torn / shall be [in] torn [condition]"; ferumim is from paramt ("to tear"); the passive plural participle: "torn things"; yihyu ferumim = "they shall be torn / shall be in the state of being torn"; the tearing of garments is a standard biblical sign of mourning and catastrophe (Gen 37:29, 34; 44:13; 2 Sam 1:11; 3:31; Jer 41:5); applied here to the metzora, it signals a social death — the person must mark themselves as in a state of mourning for their own social-civic existence; LXX: τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ ἔσται παραλελυμένα (ta himatia autou estai paralelymena, "his garments shall be loosened/unraveled"), using παραλύω (paralyō, "to loosen, to dissolve, to unravel") — which captures "loose/undone garments" rather than "torn garments"; this may represent a slightly different understanding of the required garment condition, or a LXX use of a word covering both "torn" and "loosened/hanging-open"] and his head [ve-rosho] shall be uncovered [yihyeh faru'a] [yihyeh faru'a — "shall be uncovered / shall be disheveled"; paru'a is from para' ("to let loose, to dishevel, to uncover"); the hair of the head is left loose and uncovered — unbridled — the opposite of the normal covered and groomed head; this "let-loose" appearance is also a sign of mourning and of being in a liminal/dangerous state (cf. Lev 10:6 where Aharon and his sons are forbidden to let their hair loose in the Tent of Meeting; Lev 21:10 for the High Priest); the metzora wears the markers of mourning and the liminal; LXX: ἡ κεφαλὴ αὐτοῦ ἀκατακάλυπτος (hē kephalē autou akatakalyptos, "his head uncovered") — precise, using ἀκατακάλυπτος (akatatakalyptos, "uncovered, without covering"); this is the same word used by Paul in 1 Cor 11:5 for a woman praying with her head "uncovered" — the Levitical metzora's uncovered-head legislation informs the later NT discussion of head-covering in worship, the connection being the liminal/shame/exposure dimension of the uncovered head] and his upper lip [ve-al-sefatav] [ve-al-sefatav — "and upon his lips / and over his lips"; sefatayim = "lips"; al-sefatav = "upon his lips / over his upper lip"; the text means the area from the nostrils to the upper lip (the philtrum and moustache area) — the lips are covered with a cloth or the garment; LXX: τὸ κατὰ στόμα αὐτοῦ περιβαλεῖται (to kata stoma autou peribaleitai, "he shall wrap around what is against his mouth"), using περιβάλλω (periballō, "to throw around, to wrap, to clothe")] he shall cover [ya'teh] [ya'teh — "he shall cover / he shall wrap"; 'atah ("to cover, to wrap"); LXX reads the wrapping of the mouth as the active gesture] and "Unclean! Unclean!" [tameh tameh] [tameh tameh — "unclean! unclean!"; the doubling of tameh is the most emphatic construction: the single tameh yiqra' of v.45b is presented here with the doubled exclamation; the person must publicly declare their own status — the torn clothes, disheveled hair, covered lip, and oral declaration combine to mark the person in every possible register: clothing, body, and speech] he shall cry out [yiqra'] [yiqra' — "he shall call out / he shall cry"; qara' ("to call, to cry out, to announce") — the same root used of the naming of persons and places throughout Genesis-Leviticus; here it is the public announcement of one's own ritual status; this is a remarkable feature of the Levitical system: the afflicted person is required to announce their own condition — they become their own herald; LXX: καὶ Ἀκάθαρτος, ἀκάθαρτος κεκράξεται (kai Akathartos, akathartos kekraxetai, "and Unclean! Unclean! he shall cry"), using κράζω (krazō, "to cry out, to shout, to shriek") — a more urgent, louder verb than the MT's qara', suggesting public crying rather than mere announcement].

**46** "All the days [kol-yemey] that the mark is in him [asher ha-nega vo] he shall be unclean [yitmah] [yitmah — "he shall be unclean"; qal imperfect of tame', continuous state: "he is/continues to be unclean" for the entire duration of the condition]; unclean he is [tameh hu'] [tameh hu'] — he shall dwell apart [badad yeshev] [badad yeshev — "alone he shall dwell / he shall dwell in isolation"; badad is "alone, isolated, separated," from a root meaning "to be set apart, to be alone" (cf. Isa 5:8; Lam 1:1: "How lonely [badad] sits the city..."); yeshev ("he shall dwell") is jussive-imperfect; the phrase badad yeshev conveys complete social isolation — not merely distant but alone; the metzora lives in solitary exclusion from the community; LXX: μόνος καθημένων ἔξω τῆς παρεμβολῆς ἡ κατοίκησις αὐτοῦ (monos kathēmenōn exō tēs parembolēs hē katoikēsis autou, "alone sitting outside the camp his dwelling") — the LXX unpacks badad yeshev into "alone sitting"; it also adds "outside the camp" (which the MT places in the next clause)] — outside the camp [mi-chutz la-machaneh] [mi-chutz la-machaneh — "from outside the camp / outside the camp"; the machaneh ("camp") is the organized Israelite community in the wilderness — the sacred space arranged around the Tabernacle; to be sent outside the camp is to be excluded from the covenant community, from the sacred precinct, from normal social-civic life; the expulsion "outside the camp" is the spatial enforcement of the tameh status; the metzora joins other categories of the severely impure (corpse-impure persons, those with genital discharges) who are sent outside the camp in Num 5:1-4; LXX: ἔξω τῆς παρεμβολῆς ἡ κατοίκησις αὐτοῦ (exō tēs parembolēs hē katoikēsis autou, "outside the camp his dwelling"), using παρεμβολή (parembolē, "camp, military camp, encampment") for machaneh] his dwelling [moshav'o] [moshav'o — "his dwelling-place / his settled location / his residence"; moshav is from yashav ("to dwell"), meaning "the place of dwelling, the seat, the settlement"; this is the formal legal sentence: the outside-the-camp location is the metzora's moshav for the duration of the condition].

---

**47** "And the garment [ve-ha-beged] [ve-ha-beged — "and the garment"; beged ("garment, covering, clothes"); this opens the final section of the chapter (vv.47–59): tzara'at in garments; the extension of the tzara'at-diagnostic system to garments is one of the most theologically and practically interesting features of ch.13; the same ritual examination-by-priest, quarantine, spreading-criterion, and tahor/tameh verdict applies to textiles as to skin; the LXX renders ha-beged as τὸ ἱμάτιον (to himation, "the garment, the cloak")] — when [ki] there is in it [yihyeh vo] the mark of tzara'at [nega tzara'at] [nega tzara'at] — in a woolen garment [be-veged tzemer] [be-veged tzemer — "in a garment of wool"; tzemer = "wool"; LXX: ἐν τῷ ἱματίῳ τῷ ἐρεῷ (en tō himatiō tō ereō, "in a woolen garment"), using ἔριον (erion, "wool")] or in a linen garment [o be-veged pishtim] [pishtim — "linen, flax-cloth"; pishtim from pishtah ("flax"); LXX: ἢ τῷ ἱματίῳ τῷ στυππυΐνῳ (ē tō himatiō tō styppiynō, "or in a tow-linen garment"), using στύππιον (styppiyon, "tow, rough flax fiber") — a specific type of coarse linen]:

**48** "Or in the warp [ba-sheti] [ba-sheti — [the warp]; sheti is the technical weaving term for the lengthwise threads of a woven fabric — the warp threads (the vertical threads on a loom); LXX: τῷ στήμονι (tō stēmoni, "the warp"), using στήμων (stēmōn, "warp thread") — the standard Greek weaving term, from ἵστημι (histēmi, "to stand") since warp threads stand vertically; both the MT and LXX preserve the technical weaving vocabulary precisely] or in the woof [o ba-'erev] [ba-'erev — [the woof/weft]; 'erev is the technical term for the horizontal cross-threads of a woven fabric — the weft or filling (the threads that go "across" the warp); LXX: τῇ κρόκῃ (tē krokē, "the weft/woof thread"), using κρόκη (krokē, "weft, filling thread") — equally precise] of linen or of wool [pishtim o tzemer] [pishtim o tzemer] or in leather [o be-'or] [be-'or — "in skin/leather"; 'or ("skin, hide, leather") here applied to a leather article; not to be confused with 'or basar ("skin of flesh") used throughout chs.13-14 for human skin — the same word serves for both human skin and animal hide; LXX: ἢ ἐν δέρματι (ē en dermati, "or in leather/skin"), using δέρμα (derma) for both skin and leather] or in any article of leather [o be-khol-melacat 'or] [melacat 'or — "work of leather / leather article / anything made of leather"; melacat is from malacha' ("work, craft, handiwork"); LXX: ἢ ἐν παντὶ κατασκευάσματι δερματίνῳ (ē en panti kataskeuasmati dermatinō, "or in any leather article"), using κατασκεύασμα (kataskeuasma, "a crafted article, a manufactured object")]:

**49** "And the mark [ve-hayah ha-nega] [ve-hayah ha-nega] is greenish [yeraqraq] [yeraqraq — "greenish, yellowish-green"; the reduplicative form of yareq ("green, yellow-green"); the reduplication gives an intensive or approximative sense: "very green / greenish / somewhat green"; LXX: χλωρίζον ἢ πυρρίζον (chlorizōn ē pyrrizōn, "greenish or reddish-yellow") — the LXX splits the ambiguous Hebrew color term into two possibilities: χλωρίζω (chlorizō, "to be green, to turn green") and πυρρίζω (pyrrizō, "to be reddish, fiery-red"), suggesting the Vaticanus translators understood yeraqraq as encompassing a range from green to reddish; this is a genuine exegetical divergence — the MT has one color word, the LXX offers two alternatives] or reddish [o adamdám] [adamdám — "reddish, reddish"; another reduplicative form, from adam ("red"); LXX: ἢ πυρρίζον (ē pyrrizōn, "or reddish") — as above, the LXX distributes these Hebrew color terms differently] in the garment [ba-beged] or in the leather [o ba-'or] or in the warp or in the woof [o ba-sheti o ba-'erev] or in any leather article [o be-khol-keli 'or] [keli 'or — "vessel/article of leather"; keli ("vessel, article, utensil") combined with 'or ("leather") = "a leather article/implement"] — it [is] a mark of tzara'at [nega tzara'at hu'] [nega tzara'at hu'] and shall be shown [ve-hur'ah] [ve-hur'ah — "and it shall be shown"; hiphil passive of ra'ah ("to see/show"), third feminine singular; the garment or article is "shown" — brought to the priest for examination; LXX: καὶ δειχθήσεται (kai deikhthēsetai, "and it shall be shown")] to the priest [el-ha-kohen]:

**50** "And the priest shall examine [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] the mark [et-ha-nega] and shall shut up [ve-hisgir ha-kohen et-ha-nega] the mark [et-ha-nega] seven days [shivat yamim]:

**51** "And he shall examine [ve-ra'ah] the mark [et-ha-nega] on the seventh day [ba-yom ha-shevi'i] — if the mark has spread [pasah ha-nega] in the garment [ba-beged] or in the warp or in the woof [ba-sheti o ba-'erev] or in the leather [o ba-'or] or in anything that is made of leather [be-khol asher-ya'aseh ha-'or la-melacha] [be-khol asher-ya'aseh ha-'or la-melacha — "in everything that the leather is made for work / in anything for which the leather is used in craftsmanship"; LXX: ἢ ἐν παντὶ σκεύει δερματίνῳ (ē en panti skeueì dermatinō, "or in any leather vessel/article")] — the mark is a pernicious tzara'at [tzara'at mam'eret ha-nega] [tzara'at mam'eret ha-nega — "the mark is a gnawing/pernicious tzara'at"; mam'eret is from ma'ar ("to be bare, to strip, to gnaw/consume"), qal participle feminine (agreeing with tzara'at): "gnawing, consuming, stripping"; the pernicious tzara'at is the kind that "gnaws" or "strips" — actively consuming the material; LXX: λέπρα ἐνδεδυκυῖα ἐστίν (lepra endedykuia estin, "it is an embedded/entrenched leprosy"), using ἐνδύω (endyō, "to put on, to enter into, to penetrate") in the perfect: "a leprosy that has penetrated / entrenched itself" — a paraphrase rather than a literal rendering of mam'eret; the LXX may understand mam'eret as indicating deep penetration of the textile]; it [is] unclean [hi' teme'ah] [hi' teme'ah — "it is unclean"; the feminine pronoun and adjective agreeing with tzara'at]:

**52** "And he shall burn [ve-saraf] [ve-saraf — "and he shall burn"; qal perfect with consecutive-vav; saraf ("to burn, to incinerate") — the garment is not merely discarded but burned; the fire destroys the contaminated object; the same disposal by fire is required for the ashes of the red heifer (Num 19) and other severely impure objects] the garment [et-ha-beged] or the warp or the woof [o et-ha-sheti o et-ha-'erev] of wool or of linen [ba-tzemer o ba-pishtim] [ba-tzemer o ba-pishtim] or any leather article [o et-khol-keli ha-'or] [o et-khol-keli ha-'or] in which is the mark [asher-yihyeh vo ha-nega] [asher-yihyeh vo ha-nega] — for it [is] a pernicious tzara'at [ki-tzara'at mam'eret hi'] [ki-tzara'at mam'eret hi' — "for it is a gnawing/pernicious tzara'at"]; it shall be burned with fire [ba-esh tisaref] [ba-esh tisaref — "with fire it shall be burned"; the passive-reflexive burning; LXX: ἐν πυρὶ κατακαυθήσεται (en pyri katakauthesetai, "it shall be burned down in fire"), using κατακαίω (katakaiō, "to burn completely, to burn down")]:

**53** "But if the priest examines [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] and behold [ve-hineh] the mark has not spread [lo' fasah ha-nega] in the garment [ba-beged] or in the warp or in the woof [o ba-sheti o ba-'erev] or in any leather article [o be-khol-keli 'or]:

**54** "Then the priest shall command [ve-tsivah ha-kohen] [ve-tsivah ha-kohen — "and the priest shall command"; tsavah piel ("to command, to order") — the priest issues an authoritative directive for the treatment of the garment; LXX: καὶ συντάξει ὁ ἱερεύς (kai syntaxei ho hiereus, "and the priest shall arrange/order"), using συντάσσω (syntassō, "to arrange, to command, to order")] and they shall wash [ve-khibbes] [ve-khibbes — "and they shall wash it"; piel of kabas, as in v.6 — thorough laundering; LXX: καὶ πλυθήσεται (kai plythēsetai, "and it shall be washed"), in the passive] the [article in which is] the mark [et asher-bo ha-nega] [et asher-bo ha-nega — "that which has the mark in it"] and he shall shut it up [ve-hisgir ha-kohen] [ve-hisgir ha-kohen] a second time [sheni'it] [sheni'it] seven days [shivat yamim]:

**55** "And the priest shall examine [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] the mark [et-ha-nega] after it has been washed [achar hikkabes ha-nega] [achar hikkabes ha-nega — "after the mark has been washed"; hikkabes is niphal of kabas ("to be washed"), passive: "after it has been washed / laundered"] — and behold [ve-hineh] the mark has not changed its appearance [lo'-hafakh ha-nega et-'eyn'o] [lo'-hafakh ha-nega et-'eyn'o — "the mark has not turned its appearance / has not changed its color"; et-'eyn'o = "its eye / its appearance / its color"; 'ayin ("eye") used idiomatically for appearance or color (cf. Num 11:7: "its appearance [eyn'o] was like the appearance of bdellium"); the mark has not changed in color after washing; LXX: καὶ ἰδοὺ μὴ μετέβαλεν τὴν ὄψιν αὐτοῦ τὸ θέρισμα (kai idou mē metebalen tēn opsin autou to therisma, "and behold the mark has not changed its appearance"), using ὄψις (opsis, "appearance, look, view")] and the mark has not spread [ve-ha-nega lo' fasah] — it is unclean [tame' hu'] [tame' hu']; in fire you shall burn it [ba-esh tisref'enu] [ba-esh tisref'enu — "in fire you shall burn it"; the burning command repeated, the suffix -enu = "it"; LXX: ἐν πυρὶ κατακαυθήσεται (en pyri katakauthesetai)] — it [is] a corroding [petechet hi'] [petechet hi' — "it is a petechet"; petechet [פְּחֶתֶת] is a hapax legomenon; from the root pachat ("to dig, to excavate, to hollow out"), petechet means "hollowness, a pitting, a corroding, an etching" — the tzara'at has "corroded" or "pitted" the fabric from the inside; it describes the condition of a fabric where the tzara'at has penetrated but not visibly spread, yet the material has become corroded/pitted; the distinction between the pernicious mam'eret (v.51–52: spreading, visible) and petechet (v.55: stable but corroded from within) is important — both require burning; LXX: ἐπισπεύδουσα ἡ θέρισμα ἐστίν (epispeudousa to therisma estin, "it is an advancing/hastening mark") — a very different rendering; ἐπισπεύδω (epispeudō, "to hasten toward, to advance") may reflect an understanding of petechet as "advancing (inwardly)" rather than "corroding/pitting"] — in its bald-spot [be-qarchat'o] [be-qarchat'o — "in its bald-spot"; qarchat'o here used of the fabric — the "bald-spot" of the fabric, where the material has been thinned or worn through by the tzara'at; the same vocabulary applied to human baldness in v.40 is now applied to fabric! The bald-spot of the fabric is where the material has been "stripped bare" by the pernicious tzara'at; LXX: ἐν τῇ φαλακρώματι αὐτοῦ (en tē phalakrōmati autou, "in its baldness"), using φαλάκρωμα (phalakrōma, "baldness, bald spot") — the LXX preserves the same "baldness" vocabulary for fabric and scalp; this is a striking feature of the LXX rendering], on its front [ba-qorchat'o] [ba-qorchat'o — this appears to be a textual duplication or variant of qarchat'o; some manuscripts read this as a second bald-area descriptor; MT has two separate words here: "in its qarchat or in its qorchat" — front-baldness and back-baldness, perhaps distinguishing the two sides of the fabric; LXX: ἢ ἐν τῷ φαλακρώματι αὐτοῦ (ē en tō phalakrōmati autou, "or in its baldness")] or on its back [o be-gabacht'o] [o be-gabacht'o — "or in its forehead-bald / back-bald"; the garment-bald-spot language now mirrors the human-bald-spot language of vv.40–44 exactly: qarchat'o ("its crown-bald-spot") and gabacht'o ("its forehead-bald-spot") from the same terms for human baldness; this deliberate terminological parallel between skin and textile in the tzara'at legislation suggests an underlying conception of garments as extensions of the person — subject to the same ritual categories of purity and impurity]:

**56** "And if the priest examines [ve-ra'ah ha-kohen] and behold [ve-hineh] the mark has faded [kehah ha-nega] [kehah ha-nega — "the mark has faded/dimmed"; kehah as in vv.6, 21, 28 — the diminishing criterion] after it was washed [achar kibbes'o] [achar kibbes'o — "after his/its washing"] — he shall tear it [ve-qarac oto] [ve-qarac oto — "and he shall tear it out / he shall rip it out"; qarac is from qarac' ("to tear off, to strip off, to rip"); the verb is used of ripping/tearing a section away from a whole; LXX: καὶ ἀποσχίσει αὐτό (kai aposchissei auto, "and he shall tear/rip it off"), using ἀποσχίζω (aposchizō, "to split off, to cleave away")] from the garment [min-ha-beged] or from the leather [o min-ha-'or] [o min-ha-'or] or from the warp or from the woof [o min-ha-sheti o min-ha-'erev] [o min-ha-sheti o min-ha-'erev]:

## Textual Notes

**On tzara'at [צָרַעַת] and "leprosy" (v.2):** The translation of tzara'at as "leprosy" derives from the LXX's λέπρα (lepra) and was hardened into English usage by the KJV (1611). In classical Greek, lepra referred to a scaly skin condition (from λεπίς, "scale"), not specifically to Hansen's disease (Mycobacterium leprae), which ancient Greek medicine knew as ἐλεφαντίασις (elephantiasis). The diagnostic criteria of Lev 13 — white or bright spots, color changes, hair changes, spreading, depth — do not describe Hansen's disease, which presents with loss of sensation, nodular thickening, and nerve damage. The chapter's conditions are more consistent with a range of inflammatory and pigmentation disorders (vitiligo, psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, favus, ringworm of the scalp, and similar conditions). Crucially, the diagnosis in this chapter is ritual-legal, not medical: the priest determines ritual status (tameh/tahor), not pathology or treatment. The EMET translation preserves tzara'at untranslated as the most honest rendering.

**On the three triggering lesion-types (v.2): se'et / sapachat / baheret:** These three terms form a triad of the possible presenting conditions: se'et [שְׂאֵת] = an elevated swelling or papule; sapachat [סַפַּחַת] = an adherent/flat rash or scab (possibly from sapach, "to add/adhere"); baheret [בַּהֶרֶת] = a bright or shining spot (from bahar, "to shine"). The LXX consistently renders these with three different Greek terms (οὐλή, θέρμα/σημεῖον, τηλαύγημα), but the Greek terms are less systematic than the Hebrew. The Talmud (b.Neg 1:1–2) develops an elaborate system of four "fathers" and twelve "offspring" of tzara'at based on these three root types.

**On hisgir [הִסְגִּיר] — the quarantine protocol (vv.4–5):** The 7-day quarantine (or double 7-day in uncertain cases) is the world's earliest recorded quarantine protocol. The word hisgir (hiphil of sagar, "to shut/close") means "to shut [someone] away" — an administratively enforced isolation. The priest "closes" the patient — not in a room necessarily, but in a liminal social space outside normal community life, pending re-examination. The LXX consistently renders hisgir as συγκλείω (synkleiō, "to shut up, to enclose"), preserving the "shutting" imagery. The diagnostic logic is sophisticated: uncertain cases get a waiting period to assess stability vs. spreading; spreading is ominous, stability is reassuring, fading is the best sign.

**On the body-site specific criteria:**

- Body skin (vv.1–8): Ominous signs are white hair within the lesion AND apparent depth (sunken). These criteria are cumulative: both must be present for a definitive tameh verdict without quarantine.
- Chronic/advanced (vv.9–17): Raw flesh (michyat basar chai) is always ominous. Total-body-whitening is paradoxically clean.
- Post-boil (vv.18–23) and post-burn (vv.24–28): The previous injury creates a scar that can develop tzara'at-like features; the diagnostic criteria are slightly modified (depth + hair color).
- Head/beard (vv.29–37): Yellow hair (tzahov dak) replaces white hair as the ominous criterion — consistent with observed hair-color changes in scalp fungal infections.
- Bright spots (vv.38–39): Multiple dull-white spots = bohaq [בֹּהַק] = benign (possibly vitiligo); no quarantine, immediate tahor verdict.
- Baldness (vv.40–44): Both types of baldness (qereach / gibbeach) are distinguished; a white-reddish lesion on a bald head = tzara'at, requiring the emphatic tameh verdict.

**On the behavior of the metzora (vv.45–46): torn garments, loose hair, covered lip, "Unclean!":** The four required behaviors of the declared-tameh person form a coherent gestalt of social death: (1) torn garments (torn = mourning-sign, social rupture); (2) loose/disheveled head (paru'a = the undone, liminal state, cf. Lev 10:6; 21:10); (3) covered upper lip (the concealing of the speaking-organ — speech is suspended; cf. Ezek 24:17, 22, where the covered lip is a sign of mourning for the dead); (4) crying "Tameh! Tameh!" (the paradox of speech-suspension: the covering of the lip is not silence but the herald-cry of one's own exclusion — the metzora speaks by announcing their own un-speakability). These four behaviors mark the metzora as simultaneously mourner, outcast, and public-health herald.

**LXX divergence at v.45 (garment condition):** The MT says begadav yihyu ferumim ("his garments shall be torn"), while the LXX says τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ ἔσται παραλελυμένα ("his garments shall be loosened/unraveled"), using παραλύω rather than a word for "tearing." This may reflect a different interpretation: garments loose and open rather than physically torn. This is a significant divergence with practical halakhic implications — torn garments (MT) vs. loosened/worn open garments (LXX). The MT tradition is clear: the metzora's garments are ferumim, the same root used of tearing garments in mourning throughout the narrative literature.

**On tzara'at in garments (vv.47–59) — the "garment-leprosy" problem:** The extension of the tzara'at-diagnostic system to garments (wool, linen, leather, warp, woof) has puzzled interpreters ancient and modern. The conditions described — greenish (yeraqraq) or reddish (adamdám) discoloration that spreads through fabric, penetrates it, and resists washing — are consistent with fungal/mold growth (mildew, Cladosporium, Stachybotrys, or similar) on organic textiles in the warm, humid Levantine environment. The same ritual category (tzara'at) is applied to skin conditions and to fabric-mold not because they are the same disease but because they share the same ritual structure: visible abnormality → priestly examination → quarantine → re-examination → tahor/tameh verdict. The Mishnah tractate Negaim (m.Neg 11–12) develops the garment-tzara'at legislation into an elaborate system of its own.

**On the "bald-spot" vocabulary applied to fabric (v.55):** The MT uses qarchat'o ("its crown-baldness") and gabacht'o ("its forehead-baldness") for the areas of a garment thinned by tzara'at — the same vocabulary as human baldness in vv.40–44. This is not an accident: the text uses the same ritual vocabulary across skin and fabric, treating the garment as an extension of the person. The garment has a "head" (front) and a "back," and can have "bald spots" just as a human head can. The LXX preserves this with φαλάκρωμα (phalakrōma) for both human and fabric baldness.

**On the closing Torah-summary (v.59):** The closing formula zot torat (v.59) formally completes the tzara'at legislation of ch.13. It frames both the skin-legislation of vv.1–46 (implicitly) and the garment-legislation of vv.47–58 (explicitly). The parallel closing formula in ch.14 will close the purification legislation (14:54–57). Together, Lev 13:59 and Lev 14:54–57 form the twin conclusions to the two-chapter tzara'at legislation.
